fields.label: What is your full legal name?
fields.helpText: This is you — the person granting authority to someone else. Use your name exactly as it appears on your government-issued ID.

fields.label: What is your date of birth?
fields.helpText: Month, day, and year. This helps verify your identity on the document.

fields.label: What is your street address?
fields.helpText: Your current home address, including apartment or suite number if applicable.

fields.label: City?
State?
ZIP code?
Who are you giving authority to? Enter their full legal name.
fields.helpText: This person is called your "agent" or "attorney-in-fact." They'll be authorized to act on your behalf.

fields.label: What is your agent's street address?
fields.helpText: Your agent's current home address.

fields.label: Agent's city?
Agent's state?
Agent's ZIP code?
What is your agent's phone number?
fields.helpText: A phone number where your agent can be reached.

fields.label: What is this person's relationship to you?
fields.helpText: This helps identify who you're authorizing.

fields.label: How would you describe your relationship?
fields.helpText: Just a brief description so the document is clear.

fields.label: Financial matters (banking, investments, bill payments)
fields.helpText: Your agent can manage bank accounts, make deposits and withdrawals, handle investments, and pay bills on your behalf.

fields.label: Real estate transactions (buy, sell, manage property)
fields.helpText: Your agent can buy, sell, lease, or manage real property for you.

fields.label: Legal proceedings (lawsuits, contracts, claims)
fields.helpText: Your agent can file or defend lawsuits, sign contracts, and settle claims on your behalf.

fields.label: Healthcare decisions
fields.helpText: Your agent can make medical decisions for you. Note: some states require a separate healthcare power of attorney form.

fields.label: Business operations (run your business, sign contracts)
fields.helpText: Your agent can operate your business, hire/fire employees, sign business contracts, and make operational decisions.

fields.label: Government benefits (Social Security, Medicare, VA)
fields.helpText: Your agent can apply for, manage, or collect government benefits on your behalf.

fields.label: Tax matters (file returns, deal with the IRS)
fields.helpText: Your agent can prepare and file tax returns, communicate with the IRS, and handle tax disputes.

fields.label: All of the above — full general authority
fields.helpText: Check this to grant your agent broad authority over all the matters listed above. This is the most common choice for someone you trust completely.

fields.label: When should this power of attorney take effect?
fields.helpText: Most people choose "effective immediately." A "springing" POA only kicks in if you become unable to make decisions yourself.

options.label: Only if I become incapacitated ("springing" POA)

fields.label: What date should this take effect?
fields.helpText: The date your agent's authority begins.

fields.label: When should this power of attorney end?
fields.helpText: You can revoke a POA at any time as long as you're mentally competent, regardless of what you choose here.

options.label: It stays in effect until I revoke it
It ends on a specific date
It ends if I become incapacitated

fields.label: What date should this power of attorney expire?
fields.helpText: After this date, your agent will no longer have authority to act on your behalf.

fields.label: Would you like to name a backup (successor) agent?
fields.helpText: A successor agent steps in if your primary agent is unable or unwilling to serve. It's a good idea to have one, but it's not required.

options.label: Yes, I want a backup agent

fields.label: What is your successor agent's full legal name?
fields.helpText: This person will take over if your primary agent can't serve.

fields.label: Successor agent's phone number or address?
fields.helpText: Provide a way to reach your successor agent — a phone number, email, or mailing address.

fields.label: Sign your name
fields.helpText: By signing, you (the principal) confirm that you are granting the authority described above to your chosen agent. You can type your name or draw your signature.

fields.label: Date of signature
fields.helpText: The date you're signing this document. If you leave this blank, today's date will be used automatically.

fields.label: Important: Witness & Notarization
fields.helpText: Most states require a Power of Attorney to be witnessed by one or two people and/or notarized. Check your state's requirements. Witnesses should be adults who are not named as your agent or successor agent.
